coredata.dc:title: A comparative study of structural and catalytic activity alterations in firefly luciferase induced by carbon quantum dots containing amine and carboxyl functional groups
coredata.dc:creator: Rad-Faraji, Mehrnaz
Mousazadeh, Marziyeh
Nikkhah, Maryam
Rezaei, Aram
Moradi, Sajad
Hosseinkhani, Saman
coredata.dc:description: Despite of growing interest in use of carbon-based nanomaterials as carriers of functional proteins, less attention has been paid to the effects of these nanomaterials on the structure and function of the proteins. In this study, with the aim of shedding light on the mechanisms of interaction between carbon-based nanomaterials and proteins, the interactions of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing amine (CQD-NH2) or carboxyl groups (CQD-COOH) with Photinus pyralis firefly luciferase enzyme were investigated by experimental and computational approaches. The structural changes and reduction in activity of the luciferase upon treatment with CQDs were experimentally proved. CQD-NH2 induced more reduction in enzyme activity (15 %) compared to CQD-COOH (7.4 %). The interactions CQD-NH2 with luciferase led to higher affinity of the enzyme for its substrate. It was found by molecular dynamic simulations that CQD-NH2 binds to multiple regions on the surface of luciferase. Secondary structure analysis showed that CQD-NH2 had more profound effects on the active site amino acids, the adjacent amino acids to the active site and the residues involved in ATP binding site. In addition, CQD-NH2 interactions with luciferase were suggested to be stronger than CQD-COOH based on the number of hydrogen bonds and the binding energies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luciferase
Carbon quantum dot
Protein-nanoparticle interactions
Molecular dynamic simulation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of kinetic parameters of luciferase in the presence of CQDs
Activity and thermal stability of luciferase in the presence of CQDs
Molecular dynamic simulation of the luciferase in the presence of CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research councils of Tarbiat Modares University (Grant No. 84905 ) and Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences (Grant No. 4020774 ) are acknowledged for financial support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of A) CQD-NH2, B) CQD-COOH. The FESEM images of C) CQD-NH2, and D) CQD-COOH.

A) The relative activity of luciferase (0.25 μM) upon treatment with different concentrations of CQDs (10, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/ml). The activity of untreated enzyme was considered 100 % B) The relative activity of luciferase (0.25 μM) treated with CQDs (50 μg/ml) at room temperature compared to control (untreated luciferase, no CQDs)). The activity of untreated enzyme at t = 0 was considered 100 % C) Thermal inactivation of luciferase (0.25 μM) incubated with CQDs (50 μg/ml) at various temperatures (15, 30, 40, and 50 °C). control is the untreated enzyme (no CQDs) D) The Far-UV CD spectra of luciferase (5 μM) treated with CQD-NH2 (200 μg/ml) and CQD-COOH (200 μg/ml) compared to untreated enzyme.

Three-dimensional structure of A) free luciferase, B) luciferase after 50 ns interaction with CQD-NH2, and C) luciferase after 50 ns interaction with CQD-COOH, all designed by VMD 1.9.2 software (Top and down images are different views of the same sample).
Snapshots of three-dimensional structure of luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH at different time points.

A) RMSD diagram of free luciferase (control) and the luciferase in the interaction with CQD-NH2 or CQD-COOH during 50 ns, B) the RMSF diagram of free luciferase (control) and the luciferase in the interaction with CQD-NH2 or CQD-COOH for 544 residues of luciferase enzyme, C) the Rg diagram of free luciferase (control) and the luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2 or CQD-COOH during 50 ns, D) the number of contacts of luciferase with CQD-NH2 or CQD-COOH during 50 ns.
The PCA diagram of A) free luciferase (control) and the luciferase in the interaction between with B) CQD-NH2 or C) CQD-COOH.
The Ramachandran plot of A) untreated luciferase (control) and the luciferase interacting with B) CQD-NH2 and C) CQD-COOH.

The Ramachandran plot at 50 ns time point for A) free luciferase (control) and B) the luciferase interacting with CQD-COOH; the Ramachandran diagram of 32 ns time point for C) free luciferase (control) and D) luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2. It should also be noted that glycine and proline are exempted from the Ramachandran plot.
Kinetic parameters of luciferase for luciferin and ATP.
The Van der Waals and covalent binding energies and the number of hydrogen bonds.

head.ce:title: A comparative study of structural and catalytic activity alterations in firefly luciferase induced by carbon quantum dots containing amine and carboxyl functional groups

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. Box: 14115-154, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. Box: 14115-154, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. Box: 14115-154, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. Box: 14115-154, Tehran, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite of growing interest in use of carbon-based nanomaterials as carriers of functional proteins, less attention has been paid to the effects of these nanomaterials on the structure and function of the proteins. In this study, with the aim of shedding light on the mechanisms of interaction between carbon-based nanomaterials and proteins, the interactions of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) containing amine (CQD-NH2) or carboxyl groups (CQD-COOH) with Photinus pyralis firefly luciferase enzyme were investigated by experimental and computational approaches. The structural changes and reduction in activity of the luciferase upon treatment with CQDs were experimentally proved. CQD-NH2 induced more reduction in enzyme activity (15 %) compared to CQD-COOH (7.4 %). The interactions CQD-NH2 with luciferase led to higher affinity of the enzyme for its substrate. It was found by molecular dynamic simulations that CQD-NH2 binds to multiple regions on the surface of luciferase. Secondary structure analysis showed that CQD-NH2 had more profound effects on the active site amino acids, the adjacent amino acids to the active site and the residues involved in ATP binding site. In addition, CQD-NH2 interactions with luciferase were suggested to be stronger than CQD-COOH based on the number of hydrogen bonds and the binding energies.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based nanomaterials are considered as one of the most widely used classes of nanomaterials [1]. Great attention in the field of biomedical engineering has been drawn toward these nanomaterials due to their unique physicochemical properties including inorganic semiconducting properties together with organic π–π stacking characteristics. As single entities, these nanomaterials could effectively interact with a variety of biomolecules and respond to the light simultaneously. Carbon based nanomaterials have been successfully used in biomedical applications including gene and drug delivery [2], tissue engineering [3], wound dressing [4], biosensors [5] and cancer therapy [6]. In recent decades, several studies have been focused on the interaction between proteins and carbon-based nanomaterials due to both the biosafety concerns of them as well as the demand for making functional composites of proteins and these nanomaterials. Conjugation of proteins including antibodies [7], cytokines [8,9] and enzymes [10] with carbon-based nanomaterials yields new functional materials with a vast range of properties and applications [11–13]. However, in the process of conjugation, the structure and function of the proteins may be compromised. Depending on their surface chemistry and size, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could induce different changes in structure and function of the proteins; some examples are reviewed. It has been found that thermostability and activity of horseradish peroxidase improve upon interaction with graphene oxide derived quantum dots [14]. Trypsin has been shown to be absorbed on the surface of citric acid derived graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through its cationic and hydrophilic residues resulting in stabilization of its active site [15]. Decrease of catalytic efficiency and structural alteration in P. pyralis firefly luciferase occurs as a result of interaction with graphene oxide derived quantum dots [16]. Molecular dynamics simulations of the adsorption of protein villin headpiece (HP35) on GQDs revealed the key role of the π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic residues of HP35 and the GQDs. The amount and binding strength of the adsorbed residues and the subsequent structural changes were shown to be dependent on the size of GQDs [17,18] The binding of human serum albumin to CQDs is suggested to be through Van der Waals and hydrogen bonding interactions. These interactions led to reduction in the content of secondary structures and to interfere with HSA normal biological functions [19]. Interaction of human hemoglobin and CQDs through electrostatic, hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions promote HHb aggregation [20]. The electrostatic interactions between human transferrin and CQDs resulted in conformational changes and release of iron from hTf lobes [21]. Curcumin derived CQDs have been shown to effectively bind to E protein of flaviviruses preventing viral entry into the host cells. Understanding the mechanisms of interactions between carbon-based nanomaterials and proteins improves the biological outcome of therapeutic and diagnostic systems that are based on these nanomaterials. In this study, to further investigate the role of surface chemistry of the quantum dots in their interactions with proteins, the interactions of CQDs with carboxyl and/or amine functional groups with firefly luciferase, as a model protein, were studied using in vitro and in silico approaches. Firefly luciferase (EC 1.13.12.7) is a monooxygenase enzyme that converts chemical energy into visible light by catalyzing the oxidation of luciferin to an excited state product, called oxyluciferin, in the presence of Mg+2, ATP, and O2 [22]. By excited state to ground state transition of oxyluciferin molecules, light is produced. Firefly luciferase is extensively used in molecular biology, gene expression monitoring, bioimaging [23], small molecule high-throughput screening assays, ATP detection platforms [24], hygiene monitoring [25,26], ecosystem pollution mapping [27], protein-protein interactions [28], and the measurement of microbial contamination in food and medical industries [29]. Luciferase is a potent reporter enzyme and its structural changes can be easily monitored by its activity measurements [30]. Here in, the enzyme activity measurements and kinetic studies suggested different interaction of the two quantum dots with luciferase. The mechanisms of the interactions were further investigated by molecular dynamic simulations. It was shown that the extent of the effects of the CQDs on the structure and function of enzyme heavily relies on their surface properties.

Citric acid, 1, 6-diaminohexane, and adipic acid were obtained from Merck, Germany (http://www.merckmillipore.com). ATP, D-luciferin, MgSO4, Tris-HCl, and dialysis tubing (MWCO 100 Da and 12,000 Da) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (https://www.sigmaaldrich.com). Ni-NTA-Sepharose affinity column were provided by Qiagen (https://www.qiagen.com/us/). In all experiments, Milli Q water was used.

CQDs have been synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method, using citric acid as the source of carbon. At first, 21 mg (0.1 mmol) citric acid monohydrate (CA) was dissolved in 15 ml of water and pH was adjusted to 9. Then, 11.8 mg (0.1 mmol) of 1, 6-diaminohexane (for CQD-NH2 preparation) or 14.6 mg (0.1 mmol) of adipic acid (for CQD-COOH preparation) were dissolved in the citric acid solution and stirred for 2 h to have a transparent solution. The solution was degassed and sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was placed in an autoclave and heated in an electrical furnace for 5 h at 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered (using a 0.22 μm pore size filter) and dialyzed (MWCO 100 Da) for 2 days against water. Dialyzed CQD was freeze- dried and stored in the dark at room temperature (RT) [31,32]. The synthesized CQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, Model PE-1600-FTIR), ultraviolet−visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Lambda 25 UV/VIS spectrometer), fluorescence emission spectroscopy (PerkinElmer LS45), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (Zeiss, Model SIGMA VP 500), and transmission electron microscopy (Philips, Model EM10C microscope, accelerating potential of 200 kV).

As reported earlier, the P. pyralis firefly luciferase, was expressed in E. coli BL21, using a pET expression system and purified using Ni-NTA resins. The purity of the extracted protein was analyzed using SDS-PAGE and the protein concentration was determined by Bradford assay [33].

Bioluminescence was measured using a Sirius Single Tube Luminometer (Berthold Detection Systems, GmbH) which measures the total light emitted from the reaction in 10 s after mixing 5 μl of luciferase (0.25 μM) with 5 μl of substrate solution (luciferin (1 mM), MgSO4 (10 mM), ATP (2 mM), and Tris HCl (20 mM), pH 7.8). The activity assay of luciferase was measured in the presence of various amount of CQDs (200, 150, 100, 50, 25, 10, 0 μg/ml). First, luciferase (0.25 μM) and different concentration of CQDs were mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature (25 °C). Then the luciferase substrate was added and the activity was measured. The relative activity was calculated as the percentage of the measured luciferase activity in treated samples compared to untreated enzyme. In another experiment, luciferase (0.25 μM) and CQDs (50 μg/ml) were mixed and incubated for 10 min at room temperature (25 °C). Then the substrate was added and the activity was measured at different time intervals. The activity of the untreated enzyme at t = 0 was considered 100 % and other enzyme activities were compared to it. To investigate the effect of CQDs on thermal inactivation of luciferase, the enzyme (0.25 μM) was mixed with the CQDs (50 μg/ml) and incubated at different temperatures (15, 30, 40 and 50 °C) in a water bath for 10 min. Samples were then removed and cooled on ice. The enzyme activity was then measured by addition of substrate into the reaction at 25 °C and compared with the enzyme without heat treatment. Activity of the luciferase without heat treatment was considered 100 %.

The structural changes of luciferase upon interaction by CQDs were investigated by circular dichroism spectroscopy using a JASCO-J715 spectropolarimeter in the wavelength ranges of 200–250 nm and 250–320 nm for far- and near-UV CD, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of kinetic parameters of luciferase in the presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine Km of the enzyme for luciferin, 600 μl of the solution containing MgSO4 (100 mM) and ATP (40 mM) in Tris (50 mM, pH 7.8) were combined with 400 μl of various concentrations of luciferin. The reaction was started by adding 5 μl of the enzyme to 5 μl of luciferin solution at different concentrations, followed by luminescence measurements for 10 s. To determine Km of the enzyme for ATP, 900 μl of the assay reagent, containing MgSO4 (100 mM) and luciferin (5 mM) in Tris (50 mM, pH 7.8) were mixed with 100 μl of the various concentrations of ATP. The reaction was started by mixing 5 μl of enzyme with 5 μl of the ATP solution and the light emission was measured for 10 s. All the calculations and statistical analysis were done by OriginPro 2016 software.

Luciferase (PDB-ID 1Lci) was obtained from protein data bank (www.rcsb.org) as the three-dimensional structure of the protein. Experimental data (TEM, XTD, and NMR) was used to simulate the CQDs two-dimensional structure which was created by the ChemSketch tool of ACD/LAB package (www.acdlabs.com). Three-dimensional structures for CQDs were then optimized in Avogadro molecular package. Topological information for ligands were obtained from CHARMM-GUI (www.charmm-gui.org). During MD analysis, a cubic box containing luciferase and eight randomly added CQDs were solvated using extended simple point charge (SPC/E) model of water [34]. After electrical neutralization by addition of appropriate number of ions, energy minimization was conducted under steepest descend algorithm [35]. Temperature and pressure were then coupled in 300 K and 1 bar using v-rescale thermostat [36] and Parrinello-Rahman barostat, respectively [37]. The measurement cut-off for both short range non-bonded Lennard-Jones and columbic potentials were adjusted at 1.2 nm. Finally, a 50 ns of molecular dynamics simulation was performed using the molecular nsimulation package of GROMACS (Version 2021).

The UV−vis spectra of the CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH represented peaks at about 193 nm and 192, respectively, corresponding to the π−π* transitions (Fig. 1S. A). There was an absorption shoulder for CQD-COOH at about 222 nm which can be attributed to n–π* transition of aromatic moieties and heteroatom functional groups [18,38–40]. The fluorescence spectra of CQDs at the excitation wavelengths of 270 nm for CQD-NH2 and 285 nm for CQD-COOH showed that the maximum emissions of CQD-COOH and CQD-NH2 were centered at 350 nm and 448 nm, respectively (Fig. 1S. B).

FTIR spectra of GQD-NH2 showed the strong band at 3421 cm−1 attributed to OH and NH stretching (Fig. 2S. A). The stretching bands of CH and CC groups of aromatic moieties appeared at 3059 cm−1 and 1469 cm−1, respectively. The signals located at 2931–2862 cm−1 were related to CH2 and CH3 stretching. Peaks at 1388 cm−1 and 1083 cm−1 correspond to NH and CO bonds, respectively. The strong bond at 1562 cm−1 is attributed to CO and CC bonds of carbon network. The CO stretching and NH bending of residual N-acetyl groups appear at 1643 cm−1 and 1562 cm−1, respectively [41–43]. In the FTIR of GQD-COOH (Fig. 2S. B) the bands at 3404–3200 cm−1 are related to stretching of NH, OH and CH of aromatic groups, respectively. Signals at 2924–2852 cm−1 were assigned to the CH2 and CH3 stretching. Peaks at 1585 cm−1 and 1452 cm−1 were fitted with CO and CC moieties of carbon network. The bands located at 1398 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 were associated with NH and CO bonds [18,44,45].

Zeta potential of CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH were + 11.4 and −18.3 mV, respectively, and are in agreement with the presence of positive amine groups on the surface of CQD-NH2, and negative carboxyl group on the surface of CQD-COOH. HR-TEM (Fig. 1A, B) and FE-SEM (Fig. 1C, D) imaging were performed to analyze morphology of the CQDs. The average sizes of the CQDs were determined as 7 nm for CQD-NH2 and 9 nm for CQD-COOH by FE-SEM analysis (Fig. 3S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity and thermal stability of luciferase in the presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: P. pyralis firefly luciferase was expressed in Ecoli BL21 and purified by affinity chromatography (Ni-NTA-Sepharose). The purity of the protein was confirmed by SDS-PAGE (Fig. S4). The effects of different concentrations of CQDs (10, 25, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/ml) on the activity of luciferase enzyme was investigated (Fig. 2A). The results demonstrated that both the CQDs reduced the enzyme activity dose dependently. CQD-NH2 was more effective in reducing enzyme activity. The effects of the CQDs on the rate of activity decay of luciferase were investigated (Fig. 2B). CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH reduced the luciferase initial activity (t = 0) by 15 % and 7.4 %, respectively, compared to untreated luciferase. The rate of relative activity decay was similar in CQDs treated luciferase and the control (untreated enzyme).

The thermal inactivation of P. pyralis luciferase was evaluated at various temperatures (15, 30, 40 and 50 °C) in the presence of the CQDs (50 μg/ml). As shown in Fig. 2C, the luciferase activity was significantly diminished at temperatures higher than 30 °C. The pattern of thermal inactivation of luciferase was not significantly changed by the CQDs. Conformational changes of luciferase were studied by far UV-CD spectroscopy (Fig. 2D). Far-UV circular dichroism analysis revealed that in the presence of CQDs (200 μg/ml), the ellipticities at 208 and 222 nm which are assigned to α-helices were decreased. CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH had no significant CD spectra at the tested concentration.

Line Weaver-Burk plots (Fig. 5S) were used to determine luciferase kinetic parameters in the presence of CQDs (Table 1 ). The findings demonstrated that the Km of the enzyme for luciferin had no significant changes after treatment by CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH. However, the Km of the enzyme for ATP was significantly decreased in the presence of CQD-NH2 (from 75 μM to 44 μM) showing the increased affinity of the enzyme to ATP. This could be due to the conformational changes of the enzyme (as further proved by MD analysis) or the mass transfer of negatively charged ATP by the positively charged CQD-NH2 to the enzyme [46]. The CQD-COOH had a negligible effect on Km for ATP.

Activity loss of Photinus pyralis firefly luciferase in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been reported earlier. The structural alterations induced by GQDs resulted in decreased affinity of the enzyme for its substrates [16]. It can be concluded that the net effect of the carbon-based quantum dots on firefly luciferase is strongly dependents on their surface chemistry which in turn depends on their synthesis method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular dynamic simulation of the luciferase in the presence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The simulated structures of the luciferase in the presence of the CQDs have been presented in Fig. 3 . CQD-COOH is attached to two different regions on the surface of luciferase (Fig. 3C), one at the C-terminal and the other at the N-terminal of the enzyme. CQD-NH2 bound to several regions on the luciferase (Fig. 3B). The snapshots of luciferase enzyme in the interaction with CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH is represented in Fig. 4 .

The diagrams of root-mean-square-deviation (RMSD) which measure the average distance between amino acids [47] (Fig. 5A) showed that the system reached a plateau after about 15 ns in free luciferase and the CQDs treated luciferase and remained in the steady state afterward. The mean RMSD values for untreated luciferase, and luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH were 0.42 ± 0.04, 0.26 ± 0.04, and 0.35 ± 0.04 nm, respectively. The higher fluctuations in CQD-NH2 containing system implicate the structural instability of luciferase in this condition. This result showed that the CQD-NH2 had more effect on the luciferase structure than the CQD-COOH that is in line with the experimental results.

Root-mean-square fluctuation (RMSF) represented the amino acid fluctuations with respect to their mean positions during the simulation time. A higher value of RMSF is an indicator of amino acid unsteadiness. The RMSF values for luciferase interacting with CQDs were reduced in the most regions. This reduction was expected due to the fact that the interactions were mostly hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions [48]. However, in some important regions of the luciferase, like active site amino acids, the RMSF values were increased upon interaction with CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5B, the amino acid fluctuations at the positions 10–20, 140–150, 222–229, 310–320, 375–385, and 520–530 increased in the luciferase interacted with CQD-COOH. Among them, the residues Gly315 and Lys529 are in the active site of the enzyme [49] (Fig. 6S). In CQD-NH2 treated enzyme, the amino acids in the range of 120–130, 190–210, and 520–530 showed increased fluctuations. Ser121 and Lys529 are involved in the stability and the active site of the luciferase, respectively [49]. In addition, Asn197 and Ser199 which play a role in ATP binding [50], demonstrated DSSP changes in parallel with increase of RMSF values compared to untreated enzyme. Other amino acids in both the CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH treated enzymes mostly showed a reduction in RMSF values indicating the reduction in amino acid fluctuation. For untreated luciferase and luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2 and CQD-COOH, the average values of amino acids fluctuations were around 0.16 ± 0.08, 0.13 ± 0.07, and 0.14 ± 0.07 nm, respectively. All these structural changes could change the enzyme function by affecting both the protein dynamics and substrate binding to the enzyme.

The radius of gyration (Rg) is a reliable index for the overall changes in the protein dimension during the simulation time [48]. The value of Rg was gradually increased during the time of simulation for all the three samples showing that the protein underwent some opening in its structure. Since CQD-NH2 showed more changes in Rg value and fluctuation compared to free protein (Fig. 5C), it can be concluded that this CQD had more effects on the luciferase structure which agrees with the experimental results. CQD-NH2 had significantly more contact with luciferase enzyme (Fig. 5D).

Principal component analysis (PCA) of the dynamics which show the main component of protein movements and fluctuation patterns [48] was done on the luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2 or CQD-COOH (Fig. 6 ). Free luciferase appeared as a single cluster in the range of −6, +6 (PC1) and −5, +5 (PC2) (Fig. 6A). CQD-NH2 treated luciferase showed two distinct clusters in the range of −10, −2 (PC1) and −3, +3 (PC2) for one cluster; and −2, +8 (PC1) and −3, +3 (PC2) for the other one (Fig. 6B). The luciferase interacting with CQD-COOH showed more similarity of PCA pattern to free luciferase (Fig. 6C). This data is in parallel with the experimental and other MD results and confirms the profound effects of CQD-NH2 on luciferase activity.

DSSP (Dictionary of Secondary Structure of Protein) data demonstrated the time dependent changes in secondary structure of free luciferase and luciferase interacting with CQDs (Fig. 7S, Table 1S) [51]. In addition, Ramachandran plots (Fig. 7 ) of the luciferase interacting with CQDs revealed that the conformational changes mostly occurred in the glycine allowed region which is located around [80, −100] [52]. This region contains one of the active site residues (Gly315) [49] which has been changed in the luciferase interacting with both CQDs. Upon interaction of luciferase with CQD-NH2, Ala 317, which is involved in ATP binding was moved to the disallowed region of the Ramachandran plot. Ser284 and Arg337 are two amino acids in the vicinity of active site and have been reported to be influential in the luciferase activity [53,54]. DSSP data demonstrated conformational changes of these amino acids upon interactions with CQD-NH2. Ile282, Pro288 (in the vicinity of Ser284), His332, and Pro334 (in the vicinity of Arg337) were also moved to the outlier regions of Ramachandran plot.

For more accurate analysis, the time frames which had the most changes were separately analyzed using MolProbity online server (http://molprobity.biochem.duke.edu/) (Fig. 8 ). It was found that CQD-NH2 had changed the luciferase geometry at the 32, 38, 42, and 44 ns time points which is in parallel with RMSD and DSSP data that demonstrated the occurrence of structural changes during the 30–40 ns time frame. CQD-NH2 is suggested to change some of the amino acids in the vicinity of the active site. It can be seen that at 32 ns, Pro242, Thr346, and Glu354 which are close to active site of the enzyme were in the outlier region in the luciferase interacting with CQD-NH2. As another examples, Thr346 and Ala348 (at 38 ns), Pro318 (at 42 ns), and Pro353 (44 ns) were in the outliers while they were in the favored or allowed regions in the untreated luciferase. CQD-COOH changed the geometry of Lys529 [−43, 160.6] at 50 ns time point as one of the main amino acids in the active site. Gly315, another amino acid involved in the enzyme active site, was also changed.

The Van der Waals and coulomb binding energies and the number of hydrogen bonds of the CQDs interacting with luciferase have been compared in Table 2 . The average number of hydrogen bonds for luciferase interacting with CQD-COOH and CQD-NH2 were determined as 4.5 and 9.6, respectively, during the simulation Fig. 8S(. It is noteworthy that the absolute value of Van der Waals energy for CQD-NH2 is about 1.2 times higher than CQD-COOH, and the coulombic binding energy for CQD-NH2 is 5.5 times higher than CQD-COOH. This shows that CQD-NH2 has more interaction with luciferase compared to CQD-COOH. The interaction between CQDs and luciferase enzyme is suggested to be mostly relied on coulombic interactions.

CQDs are attracting great interest in biomedical sciences. Conjugation of proteins with carbon-based nanomaterials yields new functional materials with a vast range of properties and applications. However, CQD-Protein hybrids still face serious challenges. It is known that depending on their surface chemistry and size, CQDs could induce different changes in structure and function of the proteins. Understanding the mechanisms of interactions between carbon-based nanomaterials and proteins, by in vitro and in silico approaches, improves the efficiency of therapeutic and diagnostic systems that are based on these nanomaterials. In this research, the interactions of two CQDs, containing amine or carboxyl groups on their surface, with luciferase were studied by experimental and computational approaches. It was shown that CQD-NH2 induced more reduction in enzyme activity and higher affinity of the enzyme for ATP compared to CQD-COOH. MD analysis confirmed the profound effect of CQD-NH2 on the active site amino acids, the adjacent amino acids to the active site and the residues involved in ATP binding site. In addition, CQD-NH2 interaction with luciferase was suggested to be stronger than CQD-COOH based on the higher number of hydrogen bonds and the absolute values of binding energies. The results of this study provide insights into the complex interaction of CDs with proteins. The findings also raise concerns about the biocompatibility of carbon dots.

We declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us. We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process. He/she is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs.

Mehrnaz Rad-Faraji: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Marziyeh Mousazadeh: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Maryam Nikkhah: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Aram Rezaei: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Sajad Moradi: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Saman Hosseinkhani: Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research councils of Tarbiat Modares University (Grant No. 84905) and Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences (Grant No. 4020774) are acknowledged for financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.129503.
